rules.ghunnah: Ghunnah: a nasal sound held for about two beats.
rules.idghamGhunnah: Idgham with ghunnah: the letter merges into the following nun/mim with a nasal sound.
rules.idghamNoGhunnah: Idgham without ghunnah: the letter merges completely into the following one, with no nasal sound.
rules.ikhafa: Ikhfa: the nun/tanwin is concealed - pronounced neither clearly nor fully merged.
rules.iqlab: Iqlab: the nun/tanwin changes into an m-sound before ba.
rules.qalqalah: Qalqalah: the letters ق ط ب ج د carry a brief bouncing echo when they have sukun, especially when stopping at the end of a word.
rules.madd: Madd: the vowel is elongated, by 2 to 6 beats depending on the rule.
rules.sunLetter: Sun letter rule: the 'l' of al- is silent; the following letter is doubled instead.
rules.hamzatWasl: Connecting hamza: only pronounced at the start of speech, silently skipped when reading on.
rules.silentLetter: Silent letter: written but not pronounced.

quran.searchArabicNote: Arabic search matches the literal text - inflected forms (e.g. "اصبر" instead of "صبر") count as separate matches.

learn.title: Learn to read Quran
learn.subtitle: From the alphabet to your first surah - step by step
learn.locked: Locked - complete the previous lesson first
learn.passed: Passed! The next lesson is unlocked.
learn.failed: You need at least 70% to pass - try again.
learn.deepenCtaDesc: Faith, history, fiqh and dialects in the Study section

concepts.similar: Many Arabic letters share the same base shape (e.g. the "tooth" ب ت ث) and differ only by the number and position of dots above or below. One dot more or less makes a completely different letter, so it pays to look closely from the start.
concepts.forms: Arabic letters are written joined together, like cursive handwriting. Depending on its position in the word, a letter's shape changes: isolated (alone), initial, medial, and final - always read and written from right to left.
concepts.nonConnector: Some letters (e.g. ا د ذ ر ز و) only connect to the letter before them, not to the one after. After such a letter, the next letter in the word starts again in its isolated or initial form - you can spot this as a small gap in the word.
concepts.fatha: The fatha (ـَ) is a small diagonal stroke ABOVE the letter and gives a short a sound, e.g. بَ = ba. It is the most common vowel mark in the Quran.
concepts.kasra: The kasra (ـِ) is a small diagonal stroke BELOW the letter and gives a short i sound, e.g. بِ = bi.
concepts.damma: The damma (ـُ) is a small و-shaped mark ABOVE the letter and gives a short u sound, e.g. بُ = bu.
concepts.tanwinFatha: Tanwin is a doubled vowel ending that marks a word as indefinite (like "a/an" in English). Double fatha (ـً) at the end of a word sounds like -an, e.g. كِتَابًا (kitāban) = "a book".
concepts.tanwinKasra: Double kasra (ـٍ) at the end of a word sounds like -in and also marks indefiniteness, e.g. خَيْرٍ (khayrin) = "of something good".
concepts.tanwinDamma: Double damma (ـٌ) at the end of a word sounds like -un and marks indefiniteness, e.g. نُورٌ (nūrun) = "a light".
concepts.sukun: The sukun (small circle ـْ above the letter) means: no vowel - the letter is pronounced without a vowel and closes the syllable, e.g. قَدْ (qad).
concepts.shadda: The shadda (small w-shaped mark ـّ above the letter) doubles the letter: it is pronounced with emphasis and held longer, e.g. رَبَّ (rabba) instead of raba.
concepts.maddAlif: An alif (ا) after a fatha lengthens the a sound to roughly double duration: ā, e.g. قَالَ (qāla). This lengthening is a core part of correct recitation (tajwīd).
concepts.maddWaw: A waw (و) after a damma lengthens the u sound to roughly double duration: ū, e.g. نُور (nūr).
concepts.maddYa: A ya (ي) after a kasra lengthens the i sound to roughly double duration: ī, e.g. دِين (dīn).
concepts.hamza: The hamza (ء) is the glottal stop - in English it's the catch you hear in "uh-oh". It can stand alone or sit on a carrier letter (alif, waw, or ya).
concepts.hamzaAlif: When the hamza sits above the alif (أ), it is followed by an a or u sound at the start of a word. When it sits below the alif (إ), it is followed by an i sound.
concepts.taMarbuta: The ta marbuta (ة) appears only at the end of a word, mostly on feminine nouns. When pausing it sounds like -a; when connected to the next word in speech it sounds like -at.
concepts.alifMaqsura: The alif maqsura (ى) looks like a ya without dots, but it is pronounced like a regular alif, as a long ā - don't confuse it with a ya sound.
concepts.lamAlif: Lam (ل) and alif (ا) merge into their own ligature لا (lā) - the two letters are written fused together, and this combination is extremely common (e.g. in the word for "no/not", lā).
concepts.sunLetters: There are 14 sun letters: ت ث د ذ ر ز س ش ص ض ط ظ ل ن. When one of these follows the article "al-", the L merges completely into it and is doubled (shadda) - you don't pronounce the L at all. Example: al-shams becomes ash-shams, al-nur becomes an-nur.
concepts.moonLetters: The remaining 14 letters are moon letters: ء ب ج ح خ ع غ ف ق ك م ه و ي. After these, the L of the article "al-" stays clearly audible - no merging happens. Examples: al-qamar, al-kitab, al-bayt.
concepts.wordByWordRecap: Now read the whole verse in one go - all the words you just learned individually come together to form the complete sentence.

easeVerse.arabic: وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ
easeVerse.title: The Quran is easy to learn
easeVerse.translation: And We have certainly made the Quran easy to remember (i.e. to learn and internalize). So is there any who will be reminded (i.e. who makes the effort)?
easeVerse.reference: Surah Al-Qamar (54), verse 17 – repeated in 22, 32 and 40
easeVerse.explanation: Ibn Kathir explains: Allah has made reading, memorizing and understanding the Quran easy for anyone who sincerely tries – regardless of age or language. All it takes is the first step and a little effort.

learn.podcastOptional: Optional — listen first or start right away

nikah.title: Marriage & Family Quiz

puzzle.title: Sentence puzzle
puzzle.hint: Put the words in the correct order

matchingGame.title: Match pairs
matchingGame.hint: Match each word with its transliteration

listen.title: Listen & choose
listen.unavailableReading: This exercise needs sound and is hidden while the exercise style is set to reading.

mistakes.title: My mistakes
mistakes.clearedHint: Correctly answered questions were removed from the list.

quizMode.title: Quiz
quizMode.what: You get a question with 3-4 answers and tap the correct one. A wrong answer automatically lands in your mistakes collection.
quizMode.why: Actively recalling an answer sticks far better than just reading - you immediately see what you already know and what still needs work.

puzzle.title: Sentence puzzle
puzzle.what: An Arabic sentence is scrambled into individual words. Tap the words in order until the sentence is put back together correctly.
puzzle.why: Arabic word order often follows different rules than English. Reconstructing it yourself embeds the grammar far better than just reading a sentence.

matching.title: Match pairs
matching.what: Arabic words appear on the left, their transliteration on the right - both in random order. Tap a word on the left, then its matching transliteration on the right.
matching.why: Deliberately matching trains you to connect Arabic script directly with its sound - a core skill for reading fluently without relying on transliteration.

listen.title: Listen & choose
listen.what: You listen to an Arabic word or syllable and pick the correct word from 3-4 Arabic options.
listen.why: Arabic has sounds that don't exist in English. Only listening and reproducing them trains your ear for these differences - reading alone won't do it.

duel.title: Quiz duel
duel.what: You compete on the same questions: either taking turns on one device, or each on your own time - you play your round, send the code to a friend, they import it and try to beat your score.
duel.why: Competition motivates you to review what you're supposed to already know - and the code mode works even when you don't have time at the same moment.

mistakes.title: My mistakes
mistakes.what: Every question you answered wrong in a quiz automatically shows up here again. Answer it correctly and it disappears from the collection.
mistakes.why: Knowledge sticks best when you repeat exactly the gaps you haven't closed yet - instead of reviewing everything at random.

placement.title: Placement test
placement.what: You answer short question blocks for consecutive milestones of the course. Pass a block and it moves on automatically - the test ends at the first block you don't pass.
placement.why: This finds your actual knowledge level in a few minutes, instead of wasting time on lesson 1 with things you already know.

write.title: Practice writing
write.what: Draw the shown letter with your finger from memory - the template stays hidden. Only when you tap “Compare” does the real shape overlay your drawing, semi-transparent.
write.why: Tracing a visible template barely sticks, because your brain never has to recall anything. Only recalling from memory - and honestly checking afterward - makes the letter shape truly stick.

hifz.title: Memorization
hifz.subtitle: Verse by verse: listen, hide, recite, tick off
hifz.reciteFromReaderHint: Tap "Recite" to check your recitation of this verse.
hifz.micMissedHint: Highlighted words were not recognized - pronounce them extra clearly on your next try.
hifz.micNote: The check compares words. It cannot judge fine tajweed details (madd length, ghunnah) - for that you need a teacher.

letterTips.qaf: ق comes from deep at the uvula - darker than ك
letterTips.kaf: ك comes further forward from the back of the tongue - lighter than ق
letterTips.hha: ح is a pressed breath from the middle of the throat, without friction noise
letterTips.kha: خ rubs at the back of the palate - like ch in Scottish 'loch'
letterTips.ha: ه is a light h as in 'house'
letterTips.ain: ع is a pressed throat sound - the throat audibly narrows
letterTips.hamza: ء is a short glottal stop as in 'uh-oh'
letterTips.dhal: ذ is a soft th (tongue at the teeth) as in English 'this'
letterTips.zay: ز is a voiced s as in 'rose'
letterTips.zza: ظ is the heavy, emphatic counterpart of ذ - tongue at the teeth, full mouth
letterTips.sin: س is a bright, sharp s
letterTips.sad: ص is the dark, emphatic s - the back of the mouth fills
letterTips.ta: ت is a light t at the upper teeth
letterTips.tta: ط is the dark, emphatic t - with a full mouth
letterTips.dal: د is a light d at the upper teeth
letterTips.dad: ض comes from the side of the tongue at the molars - dark and full
letterTips.ghain: غ is a rolled palate r like the French r

hifz.tapWordHint: Tap a highlighted word to see what was recognized

gapTest.title: Memorization Test
gapTest.noKnownVerses: No verses marked "I know it" yet - learn a few verses first, then you can quiz yourself here without sound.

speechError.modelInitMsg: The speech model could not be loaded. It was reset — download it again.
speechError.transcribeMsg: Analysis of the recording failed. Please try again.
speechError.noSpeechMsg: No speech was detected. Recite a little louder and closer to the microphone.
speechError.unknownMsg: An unexpected error occurred. Open the details and share the message.

lateNotif.batteryPromptBody: So prayer time reminders arrive on time, Salati should be excluded from your device's battery optimization.

recitationModel.title: Recitation model

taraweeh.title: Taraweeh tracker
taraweeh.desc: Enter how many rak'ahs of Taraweeh you prayed tonight.

fasting.title: Fasting
fasting.markToday: Fasted today? Tap to check off
fasting.recommendedText: Ramadan (obligatory) · Mondays & Thursdays · the “white days” (13th–15th of each Hijri month) · Day of Arafah · Ashura.

qada.title: Qada counter
qada.desc: Enter how many Ramadan days you still owe and count them down as you make them up. Purely manual - not calculated automatically from your daily tracker.

khatmah.title: Khatmah plan
khatmah.subtitle: The whole Quran at your pace - choose the number of days
khatmah.customLabel: Or your own number of days (1-365):

zakat.title: Zakat calculator
zakat.subtitle: 2.5% on zakatable wealth above the nisab
zakat.belowNisab: Below the nisab - no zakat due.
zakat.note: Simplified calculator (nisab at 85 g gold). For special cases (agriculture, livestock, pensions) ask a knowledgeable person.

reminder.title: Yearly reminder
reminder.desc: Zakat is due after a full Hijri year from when your wealth reached the nisab - set the date when it did.

zakat.goldPriceRef: Use ≈ {price} {currency}/g (as of July 2026 - please verify the current price)
zakat.priceMissing: Enter the gold price to calculate whether zakat is due.
zakat.fitrLinkDesc: Separate obligation per household member, due at the end of Ramadan

zakatFitr.title: Zakat al-Fitr
zakatFitr.subtitle: Fixed per-person amount due at the end of Ramadan - independent of wealth zakat
zakatFitr.intro: Zakat al-Fitr is a separate obligation from the wealth zakat above. It is a fixed amount per household member, due before the Eid prayer.
zakatFitr.disclaimer: The amount per person depends on your country and school of thought (roughly the value of 2.5-3 kg of a staple food). If unsure, ask your local mosque.
zakatFitr.amountMissing: Enter an amount per person to calculate the total.

names.title: 99 Names of Allah

names.quizResult: Round finished - each round asks 10 new random questions.

halal.title: Halal nearby
halal.empty: No halal places found within 5 km - OSM data may be sparse here.

scanner.title: Halal Scanner
scanner.subtitle: Scan a barcode to check ingredients against common halal/haram markers.
scanner.scanPrompt: Point the camera at the barcode
scanner.notFound: Product not found in the Open Food Facts database.
scanner.fetchError: Product data could not be loaded. Check your connection and try again.
scanner.webNotice: Camera barcode scanning is available in the mobile app. On the web, please enter the barcode manually.
scanner.cameraPermissionDenied: Camera access was denied. Enable it in your device settings to scan barcodes.

statusHints.halal: No flagged ingredients found in the available data.
statusHints.haram: Contains an ingredient considered clearly not permissible.
statusHints.mashbooh: Religiously disputed - check the source if in doubt.

reasons.alcohol: Contains alcohol/ethanol - not permitted even when used only as a flavour carrier.
reasons.pork: Contains pork or a pork derivative (e.g. gelatin, lard, rennet, enzyme from pig).
reasons.gelatinUnknownOrigin: Gelatin with no stated animal origin - could come from beef, fish, or pork.
reasons.animalRennetUnknown: Animal rennet with no proof of halal-compliant slaughter.
reasons.carmine: Carmine/cochineal (E120) - a red dye made from insects, debated among scholars.
reasons.lCysteine: L-cysteine (E920) - often derived from feathers or hair, origin usually undeclared.
reasons.monoDiglycerides: Mono-/diglycerides (E471) or similar emulsifiers - animal or plant origin often unclear.
reasons.vanillaExtractAlcohol: Vanilla extract is commonly made using alcohol as a solvent.

scanner.disclaimerBody: This scanner is NOT a certified halal inspection. The result comes from automatically matching public ingredient data (Open Food Facts) against a list of known terms - it can be wrong and does not replace an official certification. For products that matter to you, please verify the origin yourself or contact the manufacturer.

prayer.unavailable: Prayer times are currently unavailable (offline & no cache).

travel.qasrDesc: As a traveler you may shorten Dhuhr, Asr and Isha to 2 rak'ahs (Qasr), and combine Dhuhr with Asr or Maghrib with Isha (Jam').

qibla.calibrateHint: Wrong direction? Calibrate the compass: move your phone in a figure-8 a few times, away from metal/magnets.
qibla.accuracyDisclaimer: The direction is based on your device's magnetometer data and may deviate slightly due to nearby metal or electronics.

hijriConverter.title: Hijri Date Converter
hijriConverter.subtitle: Convert any date between the Gregorian and Hijri calendars.

landing.title: Salati
landing.tagline: Your companion for prayer, Quran and knowledge
landing.subtitle: Prayer times, Qibla, Quran with recitation, duas, hadiths, mosque finder, and a complete learning path - works offline, no ads, no tracking.
landing.f1Desc: Precise times for your location, compass to the Kaaba
landing.f2Desc: Every reciter and translation, bookmarks, juz navigation
landing.f3Desc: 42 lessons from the alphabet to first surahs, with audio
landing.f4Desc: 12 courses from aqida to grammar - with placement test
landing.f6Desc: Mosques and prayer rooms near you
landing.f7Desc: 14 quiz modes, dhikr counter with goals and daily stats
landing.f8Desc: No tracking, no ads, no accounts needed
landing.footer: Salati - prayer, Quran and knowledge in one app.
landing.apkHint: Direct install without a store - Android will ask you to allow unknown sources.
landing.trust1Desc: Every feature, forever. No subscription, no paywalls.
landing.trust2Desc: No account needed. Your data stays yours.
landing.trust3Title: AI runs locally on your device
landing.trust3Desc: The recitation check processes your recording on-device - it never leaves it.
landing.trust4Desc: Loaded once, always with you: Quran, courses, duas - even without internet.
landing.faq1A: Yes, completely. Every feature works without a subscription, in-app purchases or ads. There is no premium tier - what you see is the whole app.
landing.faq2A: No. Salati needs no account, contains no ad or analytics SDKs and sends no usage data to us. Settings, bookmarks and learning progress stay on your device.
landing.faq3A: The core content does: Quran, courses, duas and hadiths work offline once loaded. Internet is only needed for things like recitation audio on first play, the mosque finder and refreshing prayer times.
landing.faq4A: 14 languages: German, English, Turkish, Arabic, Spanish, French, Indonesian, Malay, Russian, Urdu, Persian, Bengali, Swahili and Pashto.
landing.faq5Q: Where do the prayer times come from and which calculation methods are available?
landing.faq5A: Times are calculated for your location via the AlAdhan API. You can choose from 13 calculation methods (including Diyanet, Muslim World League, ISNA, Umm al-Qura) and switch Asr between the earlier time and the Hanafi time.
landing.faq6Q: Where does the content come from?
landing.faq6A: Quran text, translations and recitations come from established open sources (alquran.cloud, quran.com, islamic.network). Duas and hadiths carry source references, and the course texts are editorially reviewed. Salati does not replace scholars - for important questions, please ask a qualified person.
landing.faq7Q: Is the app available for iOS and Android?
landing.faq7A: Android: you can download the APK directly on this page (beta). Submissions to the App Store and Google Play are in progress. Salati also runs in any modern browser.
landing.faq8Q: What is Salati AI, and do my questions stay private?
landing.faq8A: Salati AI answers questions solely from the Quran, the 40 Hadith of an-Nawawi, verified duas and the Salati course texts. The language model runs entirely locally in your browser - your questions never leave your device and are not sent to any server.

placement.title: Placement Test
placement.recommendation: We recommend starting here - everything before this counts as passed.

mirath.title: Inheritance Calculator (Mirath)
mirath.subtitle: Fixed inheritance shares per Surah An-Nisa 4:11-12/176 for spouse, parents, children, and full siblings
mirath.noHeirs: Please specify at least one heir.
mirath.gharrawaynNote: Special case “gharrawayn” applied: the mother receives 1/3 of the remainder after the spouse's share (majority scholarly opinion).
mirath.awlNote: Awl applied: the fixed shares added up to more than the entire estate and were proportionally reduced (a practice recognized since the rightly-guided caliphs).
mirath.unresolvedNote: Unresolved remainder - scholars hold differing views for this constellation of heirs (e.g. radd or further relatives). Please consult a qualified scholar.
mirath.disclaimer: Simplified calculator: covers only spouse, parents, children, and full siblings. Grandchildren, half-siblings, grandparents, and special cases are NOT modeled. For an actual estate division, always consult a person qualified in Islamic inheritance law.

sync.title: Transfer Progress
sync.intro: Move your learning progress, bookmarks, and stats to another device or browser - no account, no server of ours involved. We generate a code that you transfer yourself (e.g. via a note or a message to yourself).
sync.exportHint: Copy this code and bring it to your other device - via a notes app, a message to yourself, or email.
sync.importSuccess: Progress restored! Restart the app so everything updates everywhere.
sync.importError: This code is invalid or damaged.

impressum.title: Legal Notice
impressum.subtitle: Information pursuant to § 5 TMG (German Telemedia Act)
impressum.smallBusinessValue: No VAT is charged pursuant to § 19 of the German VAT Act (Kleinunternehmerregelung).
impressum.responsibleText: Responsible pursuant to § 18(2) MStV: Domenic Moran, Heidelberger Str. 36, 12059 Berlin, Germany.
impressum.disputeEuText: The European Commission provides a platform for online dispute resolution (ODR): https://ec.europa.eu/consumers/odr/
impressum.disputeVsbgText: We are not willing or obliged to participate in dispute resolution proceedings before a consumer arbitration board.
impressum.liabilityContentText: As a service provider, we are responsible for our own content in this app under general law pursuant to § 7(1) TMG. However, pursuant to §§ 8 to 10 TMG, we are not obliged to monitor transmitted or stored third-party information or to investigate circumstances indicating illegal activity. Upon becoming aware of any such legal violations, we will remove the affected content promptly.
impressum.liabilityLinksText: This app loads Quran, hadith, and mosque data from external, public sources (see Privacy Policy). The respective providers are responsible for the accuracy of this third-party content; we review it to the best of our knowledge but cannot continuously monitor every change.
impressum.copyrightText: Content created by the app operators is subject to German copyright law. Reproduction, editing, distribution, and any kind of use beyond the scope of copyright law require written consent.

datenschutz.title: Privacy Policy
datenschutz.subtitle: Information pursuant to Art. 13 GDPR
datenschutz.intro: This app processes as little personal data as possible. There are no user accounts, no advertising, no tracking, and no analytics software - all learning data and settings remain stored exclusively on your device.
datenschutz.controllerText: Domenic Moran, Heidelberger Str. 36, 12059 Berlin, Germany. See Legal Notice for contact details.
datenschutz.locationText: For prayer times, the Qibla direction, and the mosque finder, the app optionally requests your location ("while using the app" only). For the mosque finder, your coordinates are sent to the public OpenStreetMap Overpass service to find nearby mosques - without any further association with you personally. You can always decline location access and enter a city manually instead (this sends your search text to the public OpenStreetMap Nominatim service).
datenschutz.notificationsText: Prayer-time reminders are scheduled exclusively locally on your device - there is no push server and no transmission to us or any third party.
datenschutz.externalApisText: The app loads Quran text, translations, recitation audio, tafsir, and hadith from public, unauthenticated APIs (including api.alquran.cloud, api.quran.com, cdn.jsdelivr.net, audio.qurancdn.com). As with any internet request, your IP address is technically transmitted to the respective provider - we do not process any data beyond that.
datenschutz.storageText: Learning progress, bookmarks, statistics, and settings are stored exclusively on your device (or in browser storage on the web version). We do not operate our own server that receives or stores this data.
datenschutz.syncText: The "Transfer Progress" feature generates a code exclusively on your device. You transfer this code yourself (e.g. via a note or a message to yourself) - we never receive, see, or store this code at any point.
datenschutz.rightsText: Since we do not store personal data on our own servers, most data-subject rights (access, erasure, rectification under Art. 15-21 GDPR) are technically moot - there is simply nothing we hold about you. For questions, you can reach us via the contact details in the Legal Notice.
datenschutz.cookiesText: This app and its web version use no cookies, no analytics software (e.g. Google Analytics), and no advertising networks.
datenschutz.changesText: We will update this privacy policy whenever data processing in the app changes. The current version is always available within the app.

agb.title: Terms of Use
agb.subtitle: Terms and conditions for using the app
agb.intro: By using this app you agree to the following terms of use. They supplement the Legal Notice and the Privacy Policy.
agb.scopeText: These terms of use apply to the use of the Salati (Salatibox) app on iOS, Android, and the web by private individuals.
agb.freeUseText: The app is completely free to use. No user account is required, and there are no in-app purchases, subscriptions, or advertising.
agb.religiousContentText: We provide prayer-time calculations, Quran translations, tafsir, and hadith to the best of our knowledge and using recognized calculation methods and sources - but without any guarantee of absolute religious or scholarly accuracy. If you are unsure, especially on detailed religious questions, we recommend consulting local scholars or your local mosque.
agb.externalSourcesText: Quran text, recitations, hadith, map data, and location services are partly provided by external, public third-party services (see Privacy Policy). We do not guarantee their availability, completeness, or accuracy; we cannot guarantee uninterrupted operation of the app at all times.
agb.personalUseText: Downloaded content - such as recitation audio, Quran text, and translations - may only be used for personal, non-commercial purposes. Redistribution, public sharing, or commercial use is not permitted unless the respective rights holders expressly allow it.
agb.liabilityText: We are liable without limitation for intent and gross negligence, as well as under the German Product Liability Act. For slight negligence, we are only liable for breaches of material contractual obligations, limited to the foreseeable damage typical for this type of contract. As the app is provided free of charge, any further liability is excluded.
agb.changesText: We update these terms of use as needed, for example when the app's features or the legal situation change. The current version is always available within the app.
agb.lawText: German law applies, excluding the UN Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods, to the extent legally permitted.

themes.title: Verse Themes
themes.subtitle: Curated verse collections for life topics - to reflect on and return to.

patience.title: Patience (Sabr)
patience.intro: In the Quran, patience isn't passive endurance - it's an active stance tied to prayer, trust, and the promise of reward. These verses show how closely sabr is linked to inner strength.

gratitude.title: Gratitude (Shukr)
gratitude.intro: Gratitude in the Quran shifts perspective: not because everything is perfect, but because noticing good is itself a form of closeness to Allah. These verses invite you to look more closely.

worry.title: Fear & Worry
worry.intro: When worries grow large, the Quran repeatedly speaks of relief that comes, and of hearts finding rest through remembrance of Allah. These verses are for moments when your mind is loud.

family.title: Family
family.intro: Parents, children, spouses - the Quran describes family as a place of mercy and mutual responsibility, not just duty. These verses put into context why family carries so much weight in Islam.

forgiveness.title: Forgiveness
forgiveness.intro: Forgiveness in the Quran isn't weakness - it's one of the highest virtues, both the forgiveness you ask of Allah and the forgiveness you extend to others. These verses show both sides.

hope.title: Hope & Trust (Tawakkul)
hope.intro: Trusting Allah in the Quran doesn't mean doing nothing - it means letting go after your best effort, with the promise that ease follows hardship. These verses carry you through difficult stretches.

journeys.title: Day Plans
journeys.subtitle: Curated verse journeys on a single theme - day by day.

selfDiscovery.title: Self-Discovery
selfDiscovery.subtitle: Ten days to pause and reflect on meaning, the soul, and your own direction.

0.title: Why are you here?
0.text: The Quran answers the question of meaning directly: humanity was created to worship its Creator. That's not a cage - it's a compass, a fixed point from which everything else falls into place.

1.title: Your soul knows both
1.text: You were created with the ability to recognize both wrong and right. Self-discovery begins with taking that inner voice seriously instead of drowning it out.

2.title: Signs around you and within you
2.text: The Quran doesn't only point to the world out there - it points just as much to yourself as a place of insight. Sometimes the answer isn't more knowledge, but looking more closely - inward.

3.title: Trials as a mirror
3.text: Hard times aren't random in the Quran - they're part of what makes faith, and a self, genuine. What's challenging you right now also shows you who you really are.

4.title: On your way to your Lord
4.text: This verse describes life as movement, not standstill: you're on a path, with effort, but with a clear destination. Self-discovery here doesn't mean 'arriving' - it means knowing the direction.

5.title: What matters in the end
5.text: On the Day of Reckoning, neither wealth nor status will help - only a sincere heart. An uncomfortable but freeing reminder of what's actually worth striving for.

6.title: Asking for guidance
6.text: This short request from Al-Fatiha is one of the most honest self-discovery questions there is: not 'who am I', but 'where should I go'. Repeating it daily keeps the compass aligned.

7.title: Finding balance
7.text: Being measured - neither wasteful nor stingy - is described here as a trait of the sincere. In self-discovery too, maturity often isn't found in extremes, but in balance.

8.title: Purifying the soul
8.text: Success here isn't measured by outward things, but by how you treat your own soul - do you purify it, or let it decay? A very personal decision, made daily.

9.title: Closer than you think
9.text: This journey ends not with distance but with closeness: Allah is nearer to you than you sometimes are to yourself. In the Quran, self-discovery doesn't lead away from God - it leads straight back to Him.

sabrJourney.title: Sabr Journey
sabrJourney.subtitle: Seven days, seven situations that call for patience - from daily life to crisis.

0.title: The four that save
0.text: This short surah sums up what protects a person from loss: faith, good deeds - and encouraging each other to truth AND to patience. Sabr here isn't private - it's something that carries whole communities.

1.title: Answering harm with good
1.text: Someone who stays patient can turn even hostility into real closeness - one of the most demanding forms of sabr. The Quran explicitly calls this a trait given only to a few.

2.title: Patience in loss
2.text: Fear, hunger, loss of wealth or loved ones - the Quran promises no immunity from these, but an attitude for facing them: 'We belong to Allah, and to Him we return.'

3.title: Patience isn't weakness
3.text: Choosing patience over revenge is described here as clearly the better path - carried by Allah Himself, not by your strength alone. That takes the pressure off having to carry everything yourself.

4.title: Patience and remembrance
4.text: Patience stands here right next to prayer and praise, morning and evening. A reminder that sabr rarely carries itself - it lives from a connection to Allah renewed daily.

5.title: Patience and gratitude
5.text: Calm and stillness - like a wind that settles - are described here as a sign for those who can do both: be patient AND grateful. The two belong together more closely than it first seems.

6.title: What remains
6.text: Everything you own will pass away - what is with Allah remains. This surah closes the journey with a promise: patience isn't forgotten, it's rewarded according to the best of what you did.

gratitudeJourney.title: Gratitude
gratitudeJourney.subtitle: Five days to consciously turn your attention to what's already there.

0.title: Countless gifts
0.text: If you tried to count every single gift in your life, you'd never reach the end. The first step toward gratitude is simple: actually look at what's already there.

1.title: Asking to be grateful
1.text: Even the Prophet Sulaiman actively asked to be able to be grateful - so gratitude wasn't an automatic state for him, but something to strive for. That takes the pressure off self-optimization.

2.title: Gratitude as action
2.text: The family of Dawud isn't told 'feel grateful' - they're told 'work in gratitude'. Shukr shows itself here not only in the heart, but in what your hands do.

3.title: Which favor would you deny?
3.text: This verse repeats over 30 times in Surah ar-Rahman - almost like a refrain meant to wake you up. Ask yourself today, concretely: which favor have you recently taken for granted?

4.title: Gratitude multiplies
4.text: A promise with a direct effect: whoever is grateful is given more. Not as a reward system, but because gratitude opens your eyes to what's already there - and makes more of it visible.

newBeginning.title: New Beginning
newBeginning.subtitle: Seven days for anyone who wants to start over.

0.title: The first step: turning back
0.text: Sincere repentance in the Quran is explicitly the start of something new, not the end. The first step of a new beginning is rarely dramatic - it's simply this: turn back honestly.

1.title: Never despair
1.text: No matter how much lies behind you: this verse explicitly forbids despairing of Allah's mercy. No mistake is too big to make a new beginning impossible.

2.title: Before it's too late
2.text: The direct continuation of yesterday: turning back is described here as active movement - back to Allah, and now, not someday. A new beginning is a verb, not a state that simply happens.

3.title: Change begins within you
3.text: Before outward circumstances change, something has to shift on the inside. This verse places the responsibility - and the possibility - for real change directly in your hands.

4.title: Bad becomes good
4.text: For those who turn back, believe, and act, Allah promises something remarkable here: past mistakes aren't just forgiven, they're turned into good deeds. Your past doesn't have to define you.

5.title: Asking for inner space
5.text: Before Musa stood before Pharaoh, he first asked for something deeply personal: an open chest, an eased task. Your new beginning too is allowed to start with this request, not with instant perfection.

6.title: Forgiveness that carries you
6.text: This promise closes the circle: whoever turns back, believes, does good, and stays on the path finds forgiveness that carries them. A new beginning isn't a single moment - it's a direction you choose again every day.

ramadanPrep.title: Ramadan Prep
ramadanPrep.subtitle: Ten days to get your heart and habits ready before the month arrives.

0.title: Why we fast
0.text: Fasting was prescribed for the believers before us and for us, with one goal: taqwa, an awake heart that is mindful of Allah. Ramadan is not a diet, it is training for the soul. Ask yourself today what you want this month to change in you.

1.title: Just a few days
1.text: The Quran calls the fast 'a limited number of days'. The month always feels long before it starts and short once it ends. Decide now on one small, realistic goal so those days do not slip away.

2.title: The month of the Quran
2.text: Ramadan is honored because the Quran came down in it; the fast and the Book belong together. Plan today when and how much you want to read, even if it is only a few verses a day.

3.title: He is near
3.text: Right in the middle of the fasting verses, Allah says: I am near, I answer the one who calls. Ramadan is a month of dua. Start a small list of the things you truly want to ask for.

4.title: A night worth a lifetime
4.text: Laylat al-Qadr is better than a thousand months. No one knows exactly which night it is, and that is the point: it wants to be sought. Whoever prepares now will not stand at the last ten nights unready.

5.title: Pack the right provision
5.text: For every journey you pack provisions, and the Quran says the best provision is taqwa. Fasting empties the day so the essential becomes visible. Think about which habit you want to leave behind this month.

6.title: Train for the nights
6.text: Rising at night is demanding, and the Quran is honest about it: the night hours leave a deeper mark. You do not need hours; start tonight with a few extra minutes of prayer and grow from there.

7.title: Giving multiplies
7.text: A single grain becomes seven hundred: that is how the Quran describes giving. Generosity in Ramadan is not a side activity but part of the fast. Decide today what you will give, even if it is small and regular.

8.title: It has been made easy
8.text: Allah says He made the Quran easy to remember, and then asks: is there anyone who will take it up? The invitation is personal. Whatever your level, the Book is within your reach this month.

9.title: Start racing now
9.text: The Quran speaks of racing toward forgiveness and a paradise as wide as the heavens and the earth. Ramadan is the racetrack of the year. Do not wait for the first fast: begin today, and enter the month already moving.

griefLoss.title: Grief & Loss
griefLoss.subtitle: Seven days of quiet comfort for a heart that is mourning.

0.title: We belong to Him
0.text: 'We belong to Allah, and to Him we return': these words are not a formula but a truth to lean on. The very next verse promises blessings and mercy for those who endure. Your grief is seen, and it is not wasted.

1.title: Ease is with the hardship
1.text: The verse does not say ease comes after hardship, but with it, and it says so twice. Even in the heaviest days there are moments of relief: sleep, a kind word, a memory that makes you smile. Let yourself notice them without guilt.

2.title: A path everyone walks
2.text: Every soul will taste death; no one is an exception, and no one is singled out. The one you lost walked a road all of us will walk. In the Quran, death is not an end but a return.

3.title: What remains
3.text: Everything on earth passes away, and the Face of your Lord, full of majesty and honor, remains. When loss shakes what felt permanent, this verse points to the One who does not change. Hold on to what cannot be taken.

4.title: Rest for the heart
4.text: 'Truly, in the remembrance of Allah hearts find rest.' Grief tightens the chest; dhikr loosens it, slowly and gently. A few quiet minutes of remembrance today are not a small thing.

5.title: Tell Him everything
5.text: Yaqub, a prophet, wept over his son and said: I only complain of my sorrow and grief to Allah. Grieving is not a lack of faith. Bring the whole weight of it to the One who can carry it.

6.title: The reassured soul
6.text: 'O reassured soul, return to your Lord, well pleased and pleasing.' These verses are a door of hope, for the one who has gone ahead and for you. Ask Allah to grant them, and one day you, this gentle homecoming.

examTime.title: Exam Season
examTime.subtitle: Seven days of focus, calm and trust for studying and exams.

0.title: Ask for more
0.text: 'My Lord, increase me in knowledge' is the one increase the Prophet was told to ask for. Make this short dua part of every study session. Knowledge is not only collected, it is granted.

1.title: Not more than you can carry
1.text: Allah does not burden a soul beyond its capacity. The exam in front of you falls inside that promise. Do what you can today, and refuse the voice that says it is too much for you.

2.title: One thing, then the next
2.text: 'When you have finished, stand up for the next task, and direct your longing to your Lord.' The verses describe a calm rhythm: complete one thing, then turn to the next. Study in focused blocks and let the breaks truly be breaks.

3.title: Knowing is worth something
3.text: 'Are those who know equal to those who do not know?' The Quran treats learning as an honor, not a chore. The effort you are making right now has value in itself, whatever the grade turns out to be.

4.title: Raised in degrees
4.text: Allah raises those who believe and those who were given knowledge in degrees. Learning done honestly is a form of worship. Begin with Bismillah, and your studying becomes more than points on a test.

5.title: Decide, then trust
5.text: 'When you have decided, put your trust in Allah.' Tawakkul does not replace preparation, it crowns it. Prepare as well as you can, then walk in with a calm heart: you have done your part.

6.title: He is enough
6.text: Whoever relies on Allah, He is sufficient for him. Once you hand the result over to Him, it is no longer carried by you alone. Go in, do your best, and leave the outcome with the One who decides it well.

rizqWork.title: Rizq & Work
rizqWork.subtitle: Seven days on provision, honest work and trust in the Provider.

0.title: Your provision is written
0.text: There is no creature on earth whose provision is not with Allah, the sparrow's and yours alike. This does not cancel effort, it takes the panic out of it. Work today without the fear that what is yours could be lost.

1.title: Pray, then spread out
1.text: 'When the prayer is over, spread out in the land and seek of Allah's bounty.' Work and worship are not rivals; the verse puts them in one sentence. Guard your prayers inside the working day and let them give it order.

2.title: Walk the earth
2.text: Allah made the earth manageable, 'so walk among its slopes and eat of His provision'. Seeking rizq is itself part of the design. Go out, knock on doors, learn: movement is expected of you.

3.title: Istighfar opens doors
3.text: Nuh told his people: seek forgiveness, and He will send rain, wealth and children. The Quran ties istighfar to provision in the most concrete terms. When things feel stuck, this is a place to start.

4.title: Halal and wholesome
4.text: 'Eat of what is lawful and good on earth.' How you earn matters as much as how much you earn. A smaller income that is clean is a better foundation than a large one that is not.

5.title: Both worlds
5.text: Seek the home of the Hereafter with what Allah gave you, and do not forget your share of this world. The verse allows ambition and gives it direction. Work hard, enjoy what is yours, and keep the destination in view.

6.title: What you give comes back
6.text: 'Whatever you spend, He will replace it, and He is the best of providers.' Giving does not shrink rizq, it invites it. Build a habit of giving from your income, however modest, and trust the promise.

ki.title: Salati AI
ki.subtitle: Answers only from the Quran, the 40 Hadith of an-Nawawi, verified duas & Salati course texts - runs 100% on your device.
ki.aiDisclosure: AI-generated answer - not a fatwa or scholarly opinion.
ki.webOnlyNote: The native AI is only available in the app. Use the browser version instead.
ki.downloadDesc: AI answers need a language model ({size}) downloaded and stored locally on your device. One-time only - everything runs offline afterwards.
ki.wifiHint: Best downloaded over Wi-Fi - it's a large file.
ki.ready: AI ready - everything runs locally, no data leaves your device.
ki.downloadError: Download failed. Check your connection and try again.
ki.deleteModelConfirm: Really delete the model? The download ({size}) will need to happen again afterwards.
ki.noAnswer: I couldn't find anything on this in my local sources. For a reliable answer, please ask a scholar.
ki.welcome: Salam! Ask me a question - I'll find the matching passages from the Quran, the 40 Hadith of an-Nawawi, verified duas and verified Salati course texts, and answer based only on those.
